serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of dry mee hoon kway in Singapore weighs approximately 350g, comprising a bowl of hand-pulled flat wheat noodles tossed in soy sauce and lard, topped with minced pork, beaten egg, chye sim, and ikan bilis.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Hoon Kway (Dry) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mee Hoon Kway (Dry), food code F05060081. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Hoon Kway (dry). GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: fresh wheat noodles / flat wheat noodles (GI ~65) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Mee hoon kway is a hand-pulled flat wheat flour noodle with similar starch gelatinisation characteristics. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (19.93g/100g × 350g serving = 69.76g) / 100 = 45.3; however, using a standard 100g reference portion: GL = 65 × 19.93 / 100 = 12.95 per 100g. Per 350g serving: GL = 65 × (19.93 × 3.5) / 100 = 65 × 69.755 / 100 = 45.3. Reported GL per serving = 45.3 rounded; however, per standard hawker portion of 350g, GL = 45.3. Value reported as 45.3 per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 548mg per 100g; a 350g serving delivers approximately 1918mg sodium, representing 83% of the 2300mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200g, request reduced soy sauce and no added salt, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables such as chye sim to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles contribute 19.93g carbohydrate per 100g with only 1.3g dietary fibre, yielding a high glycaemic load of approximately 45 per standard 350g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 150–200g, request extra protein (egg, tofu) and non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic response, and avoid adding extra soy sauce or sweet sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 548mg/100g and saturated fat from lard and minced pork contribute to cardiovascular risk; fat content is 4.25g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~1.4g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request the dish prepared with less lard or vegetable oil substitution, reduce soy sauce to lower sodium, and limit portion to 200–250g to keep saturated fat and sodium within daily targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Hoon Kway (Dry) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of hand-pulled flat wheat flour noodles served in a dry style — tossed in soy sauce and lard rather than in soup. The noodles are made fresh by stretching and tearing wheat dough into irregular flat pieces, giving them a chewy, slightly uneven texture that distinguishes them from machine-cut noodles. A typical serving is topped with beaten egg, minced pork, blanched chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage), and crispy ikan bilis (dried anchovies), with optional chili sauce on the side. The dish originates from Teochew and Hokkien Chinese culinary traditions in Singapore and is a staple at hawker centres and coffee shops. Nutritionally, a 100g portion provides 143 kcal, 6.29g protein, 4.25g fat, and 19.93g carbohydrate with 1.3g dietary fibre and 548mg sodium. The high sodium content (548mg/100g) makes it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The refined wheat noodles with low fibre content result in a medium-to-high glycaemic load, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains gluten, egg, fish, and soy allergens. It is best consumed as an occasional meal with attention to portion size for those managing chronic conditions.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05060081) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using fresh flat wheat noodles as the closest proxy, which is clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean hawker preparation of Mee Hoon Kway (Dry) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05060081 or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 1.4g/100g based on typical lard and minced pork contribution in analogous Singaporean noodle dishes (HPB database cross-reference). Cultural metadata corrected: input incorrectly classified this dish as 'western' cuisine; corrected to 'Singaporean' with appropriate ethnic and occasion tags. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on fresh flat wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mee Hoon Kway (Dry) was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per 350g serving = 45.3. All core macros (protein_g 6.29, fat_g 4.25, carbohydrate_g 19.93) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
